coredata.dc:title: Spd-CDs-driven respiratory burst oxidase homolog/polyamine oxidase-dependent H2O2 signaling molecule engineering for salt tolerance in tomato

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots, are now considered safe, environment-friendly materials. Spermidine carbon dots (Spd-CDs) have been used as new agrochemicals for abiotic stress, but in-depth studies of salt stress remain scarce. Here, foliar application of Spd-CDs improved salt stress tolerance in tomatoes, and the beneficial effects were concentration-dependent. Tomato seedlings supplied with Spd-CDs (3.0 mg/L) had a greater height, a higher maximum quantum yield of PSII, and a higher net photosynthetic rate than controls after being exposed to 120 mM NaCl for 7 d. Molecular evidence showed that Spd-CDs promoted H2O2 molecule production by inducing the expression of respiratory burst oxidase homolog 1 (rboh1) and polyamine oxidase 5 (pao5), thus causing H2O2 molecule production and conferring resistance to salt stress. The role of RBOH1- and PAO5-dependent H2O2 molecule generation was evaluated by manipulating endogenous H2O2 levels and in rboh1 and pao5 mutants. Spd-CDs-meditated H2O2 regulation of salt tolerance could be articulated by reducing iron deficiency, maintaining ion homeostasis, and reducing root-to-shoot Na+ loading. Overall, the ROS signal molecule produced by RBOH1 and PAO5 protein was involved in the control of salt tolerance by Spd-CDs. These findings demonstrate that Spd-CDs are an effective and durable strategy to improve plant performance under salt stress, and to increase food security and quality.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Foliar application of Spd-CDs increased tomato salt tolerance. (A) Phenotypic performance with or without Spd-CDs under normal or salt treatments, Images were taken after 7 d of salt treatment. Bar = 10 cm. (B) Plant height. (C) Relative electrolyte leakage (REL). (D) Pseudo-color images showing the maximum quantum efficiency of PSII (Fv/Fm). Bar = 2 cm. The color code in the images ranges from 0 (black) to 1.0 (purple), the third leaf (counted from the top) was selected for the determination of Fv/Fm. (E) Fv/Fm values. (F) Net photosynthesis rate (Pn). The data are the means ± SD of three replicates. Different letters indicate significant differences between treatments (P < 0.05, Duncan's multiple range test). Three independent experiments were performed with similar results.

Effect of exogenous Spd-CDs on the concentrations of macroelements and microelements under salt stress. (A) Potassium (K), (B) sodium (Na), (C) calcium (Ca), (D) magnesium (Mg) and (E) iron (Fe) content in shoots; (F) potassium (K), (G) sodium (Na), (H) calcium (Ca), (I) magnesium (Mg) and (J) iron (Fe) content in roots. Samples were collected 7d after salt stress. The data are the means ± SD of three replicates. Different letters indicate significant differences between treatments (P < 0.05, Duncan's multiple range test). Three independent experiments were performed with similar results.

Effect of exogenous Spd-CDs on Fe content in roots under salt stress. (A) Fe staining in roots. Bars = 5 cm. (B) Root apoplastic Fe. (C) Cell wall Fe. (D) Hemicellulose Fe content. (E) Pectin Fe content. (F) Heatmap showing transcriptional changes in Fe-related genes detected using quantitative reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR). Total RNA was isolated from root samples collected at 0, 3, 6 12, and 24 h. The data are the means ± SD of three replicates. Different letters indicate significant differences between treatments (P < 0.05, Duncan's multiple range test). Three independent experiments were performed with similar results.

The molecular response of tomatoes to Spd-CDs enriched the biological process. (A) Scatter plots show up- and down-regulated genes in tomato plants sprayed with Spd-CDs (6 and 12 h) compared with control plants (sprayed with water, n = 3). (B) GO (biological process) enrichment of Spd-CDs-upregulated genes in tomato. (C) Heatmap showing DEGs related to RBOHs, Fe-related and PAO in Spd-CDs-sprayed tomatoes compared with the control group. The color scale and labeled numbers represent log2 fold change. aThe predicted loci Solyc05g025690 and Solyc05g025680 are a signal gene coding for RBOHC. The color scale and labeled numbers represent log2 fold change.

Foliar application of Spd-CDs mediated the accumulation of H2O2 under salt stress. (A and B), H2O2 content in shoots and roots, respectively. (C and D), DCF-DA staining of ROS accumulation in shoots and root tips, respectively. Bars = 20 μm. (E and F), quantitative comparison of fluorescence intensities in shoots and root tips, respectively. Shoots and roots were collected at 3, 6, 12, and 24 h under 120 mM NaCl with or without Spd-CDs treatment, and the accumulation of H2O2 was measured. The data are the means ± SD of six replicates. Different letters indicate significant differences between treatments (P < 0.05, Duncan's multiple range test). Three independent experiments were performed with similar results.

NADPH oxidase and polyamine oxidase were associated with Spd-CDs-induced accumulation of H2O2. (A and B) H2O2 content in shoots and roots, respectively. (C) DCF-DA staining of ROS accumulation in shoots and root tips, respectively. Bars = 20 μm. (D and E) Quantitative comparison of fluorescence intensities in shoots and root tips, respectively. Plants were pretreated with 5 mM DMTU, 0.1 mM DPI, or 1 mM MDL for 3 h, then exposed to 120 mM NaCl with Spd-CDs or 10 mM H2O2. After treatment for 12 h, shoots, and roots were collected for the measurement of H2O2 content. The data are the means ± SD of six replicates. Different letters indicate significant differences between treatments (P < 0.05, Duncan's multiple range test). Three independent experiments were performed with similar results.

RBOH1 and PAO5 were associated with Spd-CDs-induced salt tolerance. (A) The phenotype of rboh1 and pao5 mutant plants after 7 d of salt stress. Bar = 10 cm. (C) Net photosynthesis rate (Pn). (D) Relative electrolyte leakage (REL). (E), Fv/Fm values. (F), Plant height. After treatment for 7 d, phenotype, Pn, REL, Fv/Fm, and plant height were measured. The data are the means ± SD of three replicates. Different letters indicate significant differences between treatments (P < 0.05, Duncan's multiple range test). Three independent experiments were performed with similar results.

RBOH1 and PAO5 were associated with Spd-CDs-induced Fe content in roots under salt stress. (A) Fe staining in the roots of WT, rboh1, and pao5 plants. Bars = 5 cm. (B) Root apoplastic Fe, (C) Cell wall Fe. (D) Hemicellulose Fe content. (E) Pectin Fe content. The data are the means ± SD of three replicates. Different letters indicate significant differences between treatments (P < 0.05, Duncan's multiple range test). Three independent experiments were performed with similar results.

RBOH1 and PAO5 were associated with Spd-CDs-induced Na+, K+, and the Na+/K+ ratio under salt stress. (A, C, and E), Shoots. (B, D, and F), Roots. Samples were taken 7 d after salt treatment. The data are the means ± SD of three replicates. Different letters indicate significant differences between treatments (P < 0.05, Duncan's multiple range test). Three independent experiments were performed with similar results.

A working model presenting the mechanism of Spd-CDs-induced salt tolerance through RBOH and PAO regulation of H2O2 production in tomato plants. Arrows indicate ‘promotion’ and ‘T’ bars indicate ‘inhibition’.

head.ce:title: Spd-CDs-driven respiratory burst oxidase homolog/polyamine oxidase-dependent H2O2 signaling molecule engineering for salt tolerance in tomato

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots, are now considered safe, environment-friendly materials. Spermidine carbon dots (Spd-CDs) have been used as new agrochemicals for abiotic stress, but in-depth studies of salt stress remain scarce. Here, foliar application of Spd-CDs improved salt stress tolerance in tomatoes, and the beneficial effects were concentration-dependent. Tomato seedlings supplied with Spd-CDs (3.0 mg/L) had a greater height, a higher maximum quantum yield of PSII, and a higher net photosynthetic rate than controls after being exposed to 120 mM NaCl for 7 d. Molecular evidence showed that Spd-CDs promoted H2O2 molecule production by inducing the expression of respiratory burst oxidase homolog 1 (rboh1) and polyamine oxidase 5 (pao5), thus causing H2O2 molecule production and conferring resistance to salt stress. The role of RBOH1- and PAO5-dependent H2O2 molecule generation was evaluated by manipulating endogenous H2O2 levels and in rboh1 and pao5 mutants. Spd-CDs-meditated H2O2 regulation of salt tolerance could be articulated by reducing iron deficiency, maintaining ion homeostasis, and reducing root-to-shoot Na+ loading. Overall, the ROS signal molecule produced by RBOH1 and PAO5 protein was involved in the control of salt tolerance by Spd-CDs. These findings demonstrate that Spd-CDs are an effective and durable strategy to improve plant performance under salt stress, and to increase food security and quality.

ce:section.ce:para: Salt stress is a significant abiotic stress impacting agricultural production worldwide. Unlike other types of abiotic stress, salt stress is a persistent challenge in the field. Recent reports indicate that approximately 1.0 × 107 km2 worldwide are affected by salt stress, leading to reductions in both the yield and quality of agricultural products [1]. Furthermore, iron (Fe), an indispensable micro-nutrient in plants, plays a helper factor in processes such as hormone and DNA biosynthesis, respiration, photosynthesis, and stress responses [2]. An inadequate supply of Fe from food sources, especially vegetables, harms human nutritional health [2]. Although Fe is abundantly present in soils, salinity stress often diminishes Fe levels, intensifies Fe deficiency, induces slower development and chlorosis in plants, and the blockage of proton pumps by salinity further reduces Fe availability, which limits Fe uptake by plants and reduces crop yield and quality. Applying Fe-based nanoparticles or modifying iron-related genes (e.g. ferritin (FER1), ferric chelate reductase 4 (FRO4)) enhances iron ion concentrations and improves salt stress resilience in plants [3].

Plants, as sessile organisms, have evolved a range of intricate and precise mechanisms to address environmental fluctuations. One such strategy is systematically acquired acclimation (SAA), which is a crucial reaction to both biotic and abiotic stressors and is essential for both growth and survival [4]. In SAA, overall signal transmission is coordinated throughout the whole plant. A systemic transmission wave encompasses a range of signals, including reactive oxygen species (ROS), electrical signals, and calcium ions. Within ROS signaling, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and superoxide anion radical serve as a critical signaling molecule that regulates plant development and stress responses. To prevent the overproduction of ROS causing cellular damage, H2O2 is continuously eliminated by many cellular mechanisms and enzymes, including superoxide dismutase, the ascorbate-glutathione pathway, the water-hydration cycle, and catalase [5]. The propagation of H2O2 waves in plants is attributed to the activity of respiratory burst oxidase homologs (RBOHs) and superoxide dismutase in a classic stimulus-response mechanism [6]. Polyamine oxidase (PAO), similar to NADPH oxidases, which are major ROS signaling molecule generators, induces a systematic response under sublethal and lethal salt stress in tomatoes [7]. For example, knockout mutants rbohf and rbohd in Arabidopsis, which lack NADPH oxidases that facilitate the generation of H2O2 molecules, displayed increased sensitivity to salt and altered Na+/K+ homeostasis [8]. Among the AtPAO family members, AtPAO5 is the gene transcript that is most responsive to salt stress in Arabidopsis. The Atpao5 mutant showed lower levels of H2O2 than wild-type (WT) plants under physiological and high salt conditions. PAO5 was expressed in the presence of exogenous polyamines and produced H2O2 during salt stress [9,10]. Consequently, artificially enhancing the production of H2O2 in the RBOH/PAO-dependent pathway is an effective and promising strategy for improving plant resistance.

Nanomaterials not only regulate ROS scavenging but also serve as ROS-inducing agents, thereby improving stress tolerance and avoidance capabilities. For example, nanoparticles such as foliar treatment of ZnO-NPs, leaf or root applications of CeO2-NPs, CeO2-NPs seed priming treatment, root applications of Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived CDs can either scavenge or induce ROS molecules in various plants, including tomato, cucumber, barley, rice, and cotton, thus modulating oxidative stress-related pathways [11–14]. Some nanotechnology-accelerated plant salinity resilience mechanisms are primarily associated with keeping ROS homeostasis to alleviate oxidative damage [15–17]. However, some studies have demonstrated that nanozymes can trigger ROS to regulate plant adaptability and stress tolerance. These reports have primarily focused on nanoscale zero-valent Fe and silver nanoparticles, which generate ROS to enhance plant stress resilience [18]. Overall, nanomaterials can be utilized as a ‘curative’ strategy, or a ‘preventive’ strategy. More importantly, the low- or free-toxicity of carbon dots, in contrast to metallic nanoparticles, offers a promising perspective for sustainable agriculture. Nanotubes, glucose-modified carbon dots, graphene oxide, and Spd-CDs have promoted plant growth, enhanced photosynthesis, and improved tolerance to high temperatures, fertilization, and overall plant productivity [19]. Carbon dots have demonstrated a remarkable ability to eliminate ROS and increase plant tolerance to salt stress [20,21]. However, while carbon dots can trigger ROS signaling, contributing to SAA and systemic acquired resistance, enhanced salt resistance, and adaptation in plants have yet to be reported.

Over the long term, plant salt-stress responses have demonstrated that the decrease in the Na+:K+ balance is directly related to salt tolerance [22]. For example, regulating Na+ movement from the roots to the shoots is an effective strategy to reduce Na+ over-accumulation in shoots. The salt overly sensitive (SOS) signaling process is thought to mediate ion homeostasis [23]. The transporter SOS1 plays a role in the long-distance transfer of Na+ from roots to shoots, and SOS1 may also play a role in ROS-induced stress tolerance [24]. SOS2 has a role within the cell Na+ and K+ balance, and SOS3 is perceived as the salt-elicited calcium signal [25]. The Na+/H+ exchanger (NHX) and high-affinity K+ transporter are essential for the resistance to salt stress and the transfer of Na+ from roots to shoots [26]. Besides, SKOR-like voltage-gated K+ channels, NRT1.5-like transporters, and HAK/KUP/KT transporters all aid in the movement of K+ to the xylem, high levels of K+ in the xylem prevent massive accumulation of Na+ in the shoots and have a beneficial effect under salt stress in Arabidopsis, maize, and tomato plants [27,28]. Exploring the role of decreased Na+ supply from roots to shoots in maintaining Na+/K+ homeostasis and function in Spd-CDs-enhanced plant salt tolerance is the aim of this study.

Globally, tomatoes are an important cash crop and vegetable, and they are susceptible to various biotic and abiotic stresses including salt stress and temperature extremes. Salinity has a negative effect on tomato production and quality [29,30]. This work synthesized and employed Spd-CDs [19]. The positive function of Spd-CDs was determined by phenotypic performance, photosynthetic performance, Fe concentration, Na+:K+homeostasis, and H2O2 production in salt-stressed tomato plants. To explore the mechanism by which Spd-CDs-induced salt tolerance was associated with H2O2 molecules, we manipulated endogenous H2O2 molecule levels using the H2O2 scavengers, NADPH oxidase, and PAO inhibitor. Additionally, a genetic analysis was conducted to investigate the physiological role of rboh1 and pao5 in Spd-CDs-induced H2O2 molecule production and regulation of salt tolerance. This work demonstrates distinct mechanisms by which ROS influence the bioactivity of Spd-CDs and nanoparticles in plants, and how carbon dots may be used in agricultural applications to regulate stress tolerance.

Spd-CDs were created using a signal hydrothermal phase. In short, 70 mL of a solution of sodium citrate (98 at.%, Macklin Co.) was treated with ultrasonication for 30 min after 2 g of Spd (Sigma-Aldrich, S2501-5G, Burlington, MA, USA) was added. The suspension was placed in a stainless-steel autoclave lined with Teflon and heated to 160 °C for 6 h. A 0.22 μm filter membrane was used to leach the cooling mixture for 72 h. Spd-CDs were obtained by removing the small molecules using a cellulose dialysis bag (100–500 Da) [19].

The online program CRISPR-GE (http://skl.scau.edu.cn/targetdesign/) was used to design the target sequences for rhob1 and pao5, respectively, in order to produce the loss function of lines. These sequences were then cloned into the pYLCRISPR/Cas9Pubi-H vector using the Golden Gate ligation technique after being inserted into guide RNA (sgRNA) expression cassettes by overlap PCR [31]. The mutant plants were then created utilizing the Agrobacterium-mediated cotyledon transformation of Solanum lycopersicum cv. Ailsa Craig. To identify rboh1 and pao5 mutant plants, we analyzed the mutation by PCR amplification and Sanger sequencing; homozygotes in T1 progeny plants were used for further study (Fig. S7).

‘Ailsa Craig’ seed from WT and transgenic plants were planted in quartz sand and grown in a controlled environment at 28 ± 2 °C/20 ± 2 °C (day/night) under a maximum PPFD of 1200 μmol m−2 s−1 with a relative humidity of 70–80 %. Fifteen-day-old seedlings were transplanted into 20 L black plastic containers containing aerated complete Hoagland's nutrient solution. After 15 d of preculture, the seedlings were screened for suitable concentrations of Spd-CDs. A 10 mL suspension of Spd-CDs in distilled water was applied to the foliage, at concentrations of 0, 1.5, 3.0, and 4.5 mg/L, followed by control or salt stress (120 mM NaCl) treatment. After 7 d of salt stress treatments, we found that the 3.0 mg/L application of Spd-CDs was best at alleviating salt stress. Therefore, a concentration of 3.0 mg/L was selected for further experiments.

Relative electrolyte leakage (REL%) was used to evaluate membrane competence according to the method of Zhong et al [32]. The Fv/Fm was studied using the Imaging-PAM system (IMAG-MAXI, Germany) described by Zhong et al [32].

Net photosynthetic rate (Pn) was measured under constant irradiation (228 μmol photons m−2 s−1, 635 nm). All of the measurements employed the fourth leaf from the top, with a standard measuring head area 1.3 cm2 and ambient CO2 values of roughly 500 ppm and 28 °C temperature, respectively [19,33].

Ion concentrations were measured as described by Suzuki et al. [41]. Plant material was washed three times with ddH2O to eliminate contaminants. The dry weight of the materials was determined dehydration for 1 week at 65 °C, milling to powder, digesting with ultrapure HNO3 for 1 day, and boiling at 95 °C for 10 min followed by 3 cycles. Na, K, Ca, Mg, and Fe were measured using inductively coupled plasma spectrometry (ICP-MS Agilent 7700 series, USA). All samples were normalized in the computation using the best-measured elements, weighted sample weights, and solution concentrations.

The PAO activity was assessed using the procedures described by Fan et al. [34] and Sun et al. [35] with small modifications. Leaves (0.3 g) were homogenized in cold potassium phosphate buffer (100 mM, pH 6.5) and centrifuged at 12,000 ×g for 10 min at 4 °C. The supernatant was used to determine the PAO activity. 0.5 mL of enzyme extract was mixed with 2.1 mL potassium phosphate buffer, 200 μL 4-aminoantipyrine/N, N-dimethylaniline reaction solution, and 100 μL peroxidase (250 U·mL−1). To begin the reaction, 100 μL of 20 mM Spermidine+Spermine or Putrescine was added, the samples were then incubated at 25 °C for 30 min to evaluate PAO activity. NADPH oxidase activity was measured using a plant NADPH oxidase ELISA test kit (MEIMIAN, China).

The fluorescence intensity of DCFH2-DA was measured to generate intensity profiles (for H2O2 detection). Detached roots were rinsed with ddH2O and treated for 15 min with 25 μM DCFH2-DA in buffer (200 mM phosphate, pH 7.4). They were then washed five times with the same buffer without dye. Root segments were incubated with H2O2 scavenger (1 mM ascorbate or 100 U ml−1 catalase) for 30 min for negative control. The leaves were removed with a scalpel, treated in buffer (200 mM phosphate, pH 7.4) for 15 min, and rinsed as stated above. Fluorescence was viewed with a Leica DM4000B microscope, and photos were taken with a Leica camera (DFC425C, Leica Microsystems, Germany).

H2O2 was evaluated in the manner reported by Bestwick et al [36] and Zhong et al [32]. Plant material was initially ground in liquid nitrogen and HClO4. Following freezing and centrifugation, adjust pH to 6.0 with KOH in supernatant, and then filtered through the column to remove lipid peroxides. H2O2 was then measured using a glutathione peroxidase/horseradish peroxidase test.

At 6 or 12 h following the application of Spd-CDs, samples were collected for total RNA extraction. RNA-seq tests were carried out using the Illumina NovaSeq platform as 150-bp-paired-end sequencing reads in BioMarker Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Raw reads were trimmed using Fastp v0.20.0, and high-quality reads were obtained and mapped to the reference tomato genome (https://solgenomics.net) using HISAT2 v 2.1.0. [37] Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were conducted using edgeR. GO enrichment analysis was implemented following Bonferroni corrections. Three independent biological replicates were performed per treatment.

Total RNA was extracted using an RNAsimple Total RNA Kit (Tiangen, DP419) and reverse transcribed with a HiScript™ qRT SuperMix for qPCR (+gDNA wiper) kit (#R323, Vazyme, Nanjing, China). An ABI VII7 real-time PCR system (Applied Biosystems, USA) was used for qPCR. The qRT-PCR primers are listed in Supplemental Table S1.

Total RNA was extracted using an RNAsimple Total RNA Kit (Tiangen, DP419) and reverse transcribed with a HiScript™ qRT SuperMix for qPCR (+gDNA wiper) kit (#R323, Vazyme, Nanjing, China). An ABI VII7 real-time PCR system (Applied Biosystems, USA) was used for qPCR. The qRT-PCR primers are listed in Supplemental Table S1.

The morphological characteristics and particle sizes of Spd-CDs were analyzed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (H-7650, Hitachi, Japan) at 100 kV accelerating voltage. Zeta potential examinations were carried out using a Malvern Zetasizer system using samples at a level of 0.1 mg/mL. Chemical composition analysis was conducted using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy on a Nicolet Avatar 360 spectrophotometer. The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a JASCO V-570 spectrophotometer. For surface elemental characterization, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were acquired using a spectrometer (ESCALAB 250XI, Thermo, USA).
The data from experiments with three or more mean values were statistically examined using Duncan's multiple-range test. P < 0.05 indicated statistical significance.

TEM analysis revealed that the Spd-CDs exhibit uniformly sized spherical morphology with a particle diameter below 2 nm and excellent dispersibility (Fig. S1A and B). FTIR spectroscopy further elucidated that a broad absorption band at 3200–3600 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of OH and NH, indicative of phenolic hydroxyl group and carboxylic acids. The peak at 1706 and 1632 cm−1 can be ascribed to the CO stretching vibration of carboxylic acids and aromatic ketone. Additionally, the peak at 1400 cm−1 indicates the presence of bending vibration of -OH. The bands at 1220 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of CO (Fig. S1C). The XPS survey spectrum confirmed the elemental composition of Spd-CDs as carbon (67.35 %), oxygen (25.94 %), and nitrogen (6.7 %), respectively. FTIR spectrum, combined with the C1s, O1s and N1s XPS spectrum analysis show the main groups of Spd-CDs are carboxyl, hydroxyl carbonyl and ether bond (Fig. S1D, FH). These results show that the surface of the prepared Spd-CDs is rich in N-(C)3, CC, CO, CC and CO. The hydrodynamic diameter of Spd-CDs was 0.05 ± 0.001 μm, and Spd-CDs possess a Zeta potential value of around 0.26 ± 0.06 mV, which contributes to their ability to enter plant cells (Fig. S1I and J).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spd-CDs enhance salt tolerance in tomato seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: To analyze the effects of Spd-CDs on salt stress, we applied different foliar concentrations of Spd-CDs to tomatoes grown with or without 120 NaCl solution for 7 d. We observed phenotypic indices and salt stress-related parameters (Fig. S2). We found that Spd-CDs at concentrations of 1.5 mg/L and 3.0 mg/L promoted growth after 7 d under normal conditions, whereas growth at 4.5 mg/L was inhibited. After 7 d of salt stress treatment, the plants treated with 3.0 mg/L of Spd-CDs were taller than the untreated control plants (Fig. S2A and B). The optimal concentration of Spd-CDs for alleviating salt stress was 3.0 mg/L; at higher concentrations, the root length, Fv/Fm, and Pn decreased (Fig. S2C-E), and the REL increased (Fig. S2F). The dose was vital for salt tolerance via Spd-CDs, as the severity of stress damage with a 4.5 mg/L concentration of Spd-CDs was significantly greater than that with a 3.0 mg/L concentration. Meanwhile, the growth of tomato plants showed that foliar application of Spd-CDs promoted growth at low concentrations and inhibited growth at high concentrations. Our earlier research demonstrated that foliar application of Spd-CDs increased the heat tolerance of plants compared with the control [19]. Thus, a concentration of 3.0 mg/L was selected as the optimal dose for investigating the mechanism of action of Spd-CDs.

Photosynthesis is extremely sensitive to salt stress, which destroys chloroplast structure, diminishes chlorophyll content, and reduces photosynthetic efficiency [38]. Compared to salt-stressed seedlings, tomato seedlings treated with Spd-CDs at a concentration of 3.0 mg/L grew better (Fig. 1A and B). Spd-CDs-treated plants had lower REL (Fig. 1C), and a lower salt-led drop in Fv/Fm and Pn (Fig. 1D-F). As a crucial photosynthetic parameter, Fv/Fm indicates the maximum photochemical efficiency of PSII. Almost all environmental stimuli adversely affect Fv/Fm and Pn, which highlights the role of carbon dots in modulating photosynthetic performance [39]. For example, carbon dots enhanced photosynthetic electron transport by interacting with the photosynthetic apparatus, potentially improving the electron transport process in Photosystem II (PSII) in vivo [40]. Exogenous carbon dots promoted growth and improved photosynthesis efficiency in tomatoes under saline-alkali stress [41]. Overall, our results suggested that Spd-CDs alleviated salt-induced damage by protecting photosynthetic capacity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spd-CDs regulate ion homeostasis in tomato plants under salt stress
ce:section.ce:para: Maintaining ion homeostasis is an important strategy for combatting abiotic stress [42]. To explore the functions of Spd-CDs in ion homeostasis, we measured micro- and macro-nutrients during salt stress. Under normal conditions, there were no differences in micro- or macro-nutrients in tomato plants, except for K content, which was induced by Spd-CDs (Fig. 2A). Previous studies have found that carbon dots can easily cross the biological barrier in plants to capture more nutrients (e.g. water, minerals, etc.) for plant growth [17]. Additionally, K+ can interact with carbon dots through hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interactions, and subsequently, the element is taken up and transported through the carbon dots to facilitate plant growth and development [43]. Under salt stress, K, Ca, Mg, and Fe were reduced significantly. Interestingly, the application of Spd-CDs increased those ion concentrations only in tomato plants under salt stress. Meanwhile, the application of Spd-CDs decreased the Na content and attenuated Na ion toxicity due to salt stress (Fig. 2B and G), and Spd-CDs played a synergistic stimulatory role in the accumulation of Fe ions (Fig. 2E and J). Compared with the control, Fe staining in the root exhibited a significantly weak blue color due to salt. Spd-CDs supplementation consolidated the blue color in the roots of salt-stressed plants (Fig. 3A). Total apoplastic Fe in the salt-treated roots was lower, and effectively mitigated by Spd-CDs (Fig. 3B). Meanwhile, Fe content was specifically analyzed in root cells, and there was no significant difference in Fe content in the cell wall between the H2O- and Spd-CDs-treated plants under normal conditions (Fig. 3C). Spd-CDs also did not induce significant changes in Fe ion content in cellulose or pectin under normal conditions (Fig. 3D and E). The cell wall Fe content was higher in the Spd-CDs-treated roots under salt stress (Fig. 3C). Also, trends in Fe content in hemicellulose and pectin were similar to those in the cell wall (Fig. 3D and E). Several genes that were previously shown to be related to Fe in tomatoes were selected to assess whether Spd-CDs-mediated Fe accumulation was correlated with a change in gene expression in the roots. The application of Spd-CDs induced the expression of several genes, including FRO1, FER1, FER2, FER3, and natural-resistance-associated macrophage protein (Nramp) (Fig. 3F). FRO1 reduces soil Fe(III) to Fe(II), then Fe(II) is transported across the membrane by FER and Nramp [44], and the up-regulation of these genes by Spd-CDs increases the acquisition of Fe in tomato seedlings, this improvement helps the seedlings grow and function physiologically better in salt environments [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: RBOH1 and PAO5 are transcriptionally responsive to Spd-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To understand how the foliar application of Spd-CDs influenced the response of tomato plants to salt stress, we considered the induction of genes related to Fe uptake following the application of Spd-CDs at 6 and 12 h (Fig. 3F). We selected these two-time points for the transcriptome experiment. RNA-sequencing (RNA-seq) transcriptomic response (6 and 12 h after the application of Spd-CDs) demonstrated that Spd-CDs attracted massive transcriptional reassembly in tomato seedlings (Fig. 4A). Using a threshold of P < 0.05 and a fold change value (log2) ≥ 2.0 relative to the control (0 h, without Spd-CDs), a total of 160 (6 h, up-regulated), 206 (6 h, down-regulated), 53 (12 h, up-regulated), and 77 (12 h, down-regulated) DEGs were observed (Fig. 4A). For plants treated with Spd-CDs, enriched gene ontology (GO) revealed that the terms “response to abiotic stimulus” and “oxidation-reduction process” were among the top 15 GO terms (Fig. 4B). Furthermore, we observed that Spd-CDs increased ROS homeostasis-related genes (RBOH1 and PAO5 in particular) 6 and 12 h after Spd-CDs were applied (Fig. 4C). qRT-PCR was performed on RBOH1 and PAO5 genes, and the expression data confirmed the accuracy of the transcriptomic data (Fig. S3). In Arabidopsis, the RBOH family consists of 10 members, from RBOHA to RBOHJ, and each has a specific function in development and stress response, with RBOHF being important in ABA signaling and salt stress tolerance [46]. In tomato, among the eight members of the RBOH family (RBOHA-F, RBOHH, RBOH1), RBOH1 (Solyc08g081690.2) is most similar to the RBOHF of Arabidopsis thaliana. The RBOH1 is strongly associated with abiotic stress response through H2O2 signaling in tomato [47]. RBOH1, which is known to be an important ROS generator, may serve as a systemic signal that empowers various forms of stress tolerance in tomatoes and crop species [47]. Recently, PAO-coding genes with approximate function were identified in Arabidopsis and rice, implying a functional coordination of ROS homeostasis, especially for PAO5 [48]. These findings indicate that Spd-CDs may trigger RBOH/PAO-dependent ROS signaling in plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spd-CDs induce ROS bursts in plants
ce:section.ce:para: A previous study demonstrated that a high concentration of carbon dots quenched ROS [13]. In this study, we applied different foliar concentrations of Spd-CDs, which affected the accumulation of H2O2 molecule; a low concentration of Spd-CDs slightly increased H2O2 content, whereas the application with a moderate concentration of Spd-CDs (3.0 mg/L) significantly enhanced the accumulation of H2O2 molecule. In contrast, H2O2 molecule accumulation decreased when the Spd-CDs concentration exceeded 3.5 mg/L (Fig. S4A). These results may be attributed to the antioxidant enzyme-like activity of Spd-CDs, which can effectively diminish self-induced ROS [19]. Foliar application of Spd-CDs at a concentration of 3.0 mg/L increased NADPH oxidase and PAO activity in the absence or presence of salt, and these effects were more prominent under salt stress (Fig. S4B and C). Applying a suitable concentration of Spd-CDs could significantly attenuate salt stress-induced injury and trigger the production of ROS.

To confirm the effects of Spd-CDs on ROS homeostasis, ROS bursts were evaluated via an H2DCF-DA fluorescence and spectrophotometry-based method in tomato plants. The results showed that H2O2 accumulation increased in the shoots and roots of salt-treated plants that received Spd-CDs compared with control treatments. Salt treatment induced H2O2 in plants, and the H2O2 content in the shoots and roots of plants in the Salt+Spd-CDs treatment was greater than that in salt-treated plants, especially at 12 h (Fig. 5A and B). The RBOH1 and PAO5 have multifaceted functions involved in ROS-dependent ABA signaling and Na+/K+ balance under salt stress [46]. Therefore, to elucidate the role of RBOH1 and PAO5-dependent ROS in Spd-CDs treatments, we examined the expression of RBOH1 and PAO5 in shoots and roots within 24 h of Spd-CDs treatments. Expression of RBOH1 and PAO5 was significantly induced after 1 h of Spd-CDs treatment, and a further increase occurred with prolonged treatment, and was highest at 12 h, subsequently lowered, suggesting that Spd-CDs mediate ROS production via RBOH1 and PAO5 (Fig. S3). Similarly, the salt-induced DCF-DA fluorescence signal was significantly increased in the shoots and roots of Salt- and Salt+Spd-CDs-treated plants, but the effect was more obvious in Salt+Spd-CDs-treated plants (Fig. 5B), which was consistent with the results for H2O2 content. The Spd-CDs triggered ROS wave was similar to that of plant elicitors (it peaked at approximately 6–12 h and became negative at 24 h), such as spermidine, H2O2, and Ca2+ [49]. This indicates receptor-like protein kinases recognize Spd-CDs, mediating RBOHs or PAO phosphorylation and establishing ROS in burst apoplasts [50]. Previous studies have shown that the importance of elicitors in prompting ROS and defense responses relies on concentration, RBOH, and PAO regulation [50]. Spd-CDs may induce salt stress responses (Fig. 5) and simultaneously take part in RBOH and PAO mediation processes by triggering signal transduction pathways, which results in a stronger, longer ROS burst. In addition, Spd-CDs are similar to functional carbon nanoparticles; both have carboxyl components capable of inducing ROS production, suggesting that the carboxyl group of Spd-CDs may play a key role in eliciting ROS production [51]. Taken together, these findings demonstrated that Spd-CDs induced the expression of RBOH1 and PAO5 and the ROS burst in the shoots and roots of tomato seedlings under salt stress.

To investigate how H2O2 molecule was associated with Spd-CDs-induced salt tolerance, we analyzed the effects of dimethylthiourea (DMTU; an H2O2 scavenger), diphenyleneiodonium chloride (DPI; an inhibitor of NADPH oxidase), and the spermine analog N 1,N 4-bis-(2,3-butadienyl)-1,4-butanediamine (MDL; an effective inhibitor of polyamine oxidase) [7] on the regulation of salt tolerance by Spd-CDs. Spd-CDs and H2O2 molecule positively mitigated stress-induced growth inhibition, reduced Pn, Fv/Fm, and plant height, and caused higher REL, but these effects were weakened by treatment with DMTU, DPI, or MDL (Fig. S5). Meanwhile, Spd-CDs mediated the accumulation of H2O2 molecules in plants, as reflected by the H2O2 content and DCF-DA staining under salt stress, and these results were reduced by DMTU, DPI, or MDL (Fig. 6 ), indicating that Spd-CDs induced salt tolerance and H2O2 molecule accumulation depending on NADPH oxidase and PAO.

To verify the role of H2O2 molecule in Spd-CDs-induced Fe accumulation, we analyzed the expression of Fe-related genes and Fe levels in plants following the application of DMTU, DPI, or MDL combined with Spd-CDs under salt stress. The expression of Fe-related genes was induced by Spd-CDs, whereas these effects were completely inhibited by DMTU, DPI, or MDL (Fig. S6). Under salt stress, the foliar application of Spd-CDs or H2O2 molecule significantly increased Fe content compared with control plants. As expected, the Fe content decreased in plants treated with Spd-CDs + DMTU, DPI, or MDL in the presence of salt (Fig. S6). Therefore, RBOH/PAO-derived H2O2 molecule production may be involved in the regulation of Fe accumulation by Spd-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: rboh1 and pao5 knockouts compromise salt tolerance induced by Spd-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the roles of RBOH1 and PAO5 in Spd-CDs-induced H2O2 molecule, the rboh1, and pao5 mutants were used. The rboh1 mutant has a 11 bp deletion and the pao5 mutant has a 5 bp deletion, leading to a premature termination at the amino acids of RBOH1 and PAO5 protein, respectively. NADPH oxidase activity in the rboh1 mutant and PAO activity in the pao5 mutant was decreased by 45.2 % and 44.2 %, respectively, compared with WT plants. The PAO activity in the rboh1 mutant and NADPH activity in the pao5 mutant were not significantly different from WT plants (Fig. S7). During growth, the rboh1 or pao5 mutants were significantly different from WT plants in the absence of salt stress; Spd-CDs-induced the plant growth was more seriously compromised in the rboh1 and pao5 mutants that received salt treatment (Fig. 7A and B). Salt had a negative effect on Pn, and REL increased in rboh1 and pao5 mutants relative to WT plants (Fig. 7C and D). Moreover, Spd-CDs attenuated salt-induced decreases in Fv/Fm in WT plants, but not in the rboh1 or pao5 mutant (Fig. 7E). Furthermore, in the absence of salt, a lower level of H2O2 molecule was observed in the rboh1 and pao5 mutants than in WT plants (Fig. S8). The H2O2 molecule content significantly increased in WT plants under salt stress, and Spd-CDs facilitated the accumulation of H2O2 molecules (Fig. S8). Nevertheless, the level of H2O2 molecules in the rboh1 and pao5 mutants was markedly lower than that in WT plants under salt stress with or without Spd-CDs treatment (Fig. S8). These findings are supported by previous results showing that the loss of RBOH or PAO function increased salt sensitivity [8]. Similarly, Fe staining in the roots of the rboh1 and pao5 mutants was dramatically lower than in WT plants with or without Spd-CDs treatment, and the increase in Fe in the roots, and hemicellulose and pectin in the cell wall due to Spd-CDs in the salt stress treatment was abolished the in rboh1 and pao5 mutants (Fig. 8 ). Therefore, RBOH/PAO-derived H2O2 molecule production plays a vital role in Spd-CDs-induced salt tolerance and the accumulation of Fe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RBOH1/PAO5-dependent H2O2 molecule is involved in Spd-CDs-induced Na+ and K+ homeostasis
ce:section.ce:para: The levels of Na+ were higher in the leaves of rboh1 and pao5 mutants than in the leaves of WT plants under salt stress, while leaf K+ content was lower in the mutants than in the WT plants (Fig. 9 ). When WT plants were grown with Spd-CDs, leaf Na+ levels decreased and K+ levels increased, and the Na+/K+ ratios were reduced in the leaves and roots of salt-treated WT plants following the application of Spd-CDs relative to plants grown under the salt treatment only (Fig. 9). In contrast, rboh1 and pao5 mutants did not exhibit an alleviation of salt-induced changes in Na+ and K+ accumulation in the leaves or roots following the application of Spd-CDs. Meanwhile, the Na+/K+ ratios of salt-treated mutant roots and shoots were not significantly difference under normal or Spd-CDs treatments. A lower Na+/K+ ratio is essential for reducing Na+ and increasing salt tolerance [52]. Thus, these results demonstrate that Spd-CDs maintain the Na+/K+ balance and are dependent on the H2O2 molecule produced by RBOH1 and PAO5 protein.

When plants are suffering from salt stress, excess Na+ is transported to the shoot through the xylem [53]. Some studies have demonstrated that RBOH-regulated ROS production plays a critical role in fine-tuning the delivery of Na+ from roots to shoots in plants exposed to saline conditions [54]. Here, we showed that the xylem sap Na+ levels increased in WT plants and both mutants under salt treatment (Fig. S9). Nonetheless, the increase in xylem sap Na+ concentrations in the rboh1 and pao5 mutants was always higher than those in the WT plants (Fig. S9). Spd-CDs caused xylem sap Na+ levels in the WT plants to decrease after the salt treatment but not in the rboh1 and pao5 mutants. The WT plants exhibited the lowest xylem sap Na+/K+ ratio after the application of Spd-CDs. In contrast, the rboh1 and pao5 mutants had the highest xylem sap Na+/K+ ratios under normal and Spd-CDs + Salt conditions (Fig. S9). Our findings suggest that under salt stress, the loss of RBOH1 and PAO5 protein decreases ROS accumulation, which increases the xylem sap Na+ concentration, thus increasing the transport of Na+ to the shoot and causing increased Na+ accumulation in the shoot and enhancing salt sensitivity. The loss of RBOH1 and PAO5 protein function suppressed shoot Na+ accumulation and salt tolerance following the application of Spd-CDs (Figs. 7 and 9), indicating that Spd-CDs-mediated salt tolerance relies on RBOH1/PAO5-mediated ROS regulation of the Na+ balance. A recent study suggested that nitrogen-doped carbon dot regulation of salt tolerance in tobacco was dependent upon limiting root-to-shoot Na+ delivery [55]. Furthermore, our results demonstrated that high xylem sap K+ content was correlated with Spd-CDs-regulated salt tolerance, and genetic evidence suggests that this is related to RBOH and PAO5 protein function.

In plants, key stress response genes in the SOS family, NHX family, SKOR channel, HAK transporter, and NRT1.5 transporters play an important role in the maintenance of cellular pH and Na+/K+ homeostasis, to prevent disturbance to Na+/K+ homeostasis by salt stress, and these non-selective cation channels also influence root Na+ influx and xylem Na+ loading [28,56]. In this study, the levels of transcripts encoding some genes for long-distance transport of Na+ such as SOS1–3 and NHX1–3 were similar in WT plants, and in the rboh1 or pao5 mutant in the absence of salt, with or without Spd-CDs. Salinity treatment significantly increased the transcripts encoding salt response genes in WT plants, and Spd-CDs further induced their expression (Fig. S10). In contrast, salt-induced expression of SOS1–3 and NHX1–3 was affected in the rboh1 and pao5 mutants. Importantly, Spd-CDs-induced expression of salt response genes was weaker in rboh1 and pao5 mutants during salt stress (Fig. S10). Under normal conditions, the SKOR, HAK5/7, and NRT1.5 transcript levels were comparable in WT plants, rboh1 mutants, and pao5 mutants. When exposed to salt stress, a significant decrease in the expression of SKOR, HAK5/7, and NRT1.5 was observed in WT plants, with an even more pronounced reduction in the rboh1 and pao5 mutants. Under normal conditions, Spd-CDs resulted in the up-regulation of SKOR, HAK5/7, and NRT1.5 genes in WT plants, while no significant difference was seen in the rboh1 and pao5 mutants, regardless of Spd-CDs treatment. Additionally, Spd-CDs application mitigated the salt stress-induced reduction of SKOR, HAK5/7, and NRT1.5 expression in WT plants. Notably, the up-regulatory effect of Spd-CDs on SKOR, HAK5/7, and NRT1.5 was absent in the rboh1 and pao5 mutants (Fig. S10). Overall, these results indicated that ROS generation was involved in the salt-induced expression of the SOS, NHX, SKOR, HAK5/7, and NRT1.5 genes following the application of Spd-CDs. RBOHs have been reported to mediate the stability of mRNA SOS, which encodes the plasma membrane Na+/H+ anti-transporter and functions in retrieving Na+ from the xylem [57]. Additionally, RBOHs function synergistically with signaling molecules to regulate xylem sap Na+ and K+ content and Na+/K+ homeostasis under salt stress [58]. Similarly, the PAO constitutes a feed-forward ROS amplification loop, which affects vacuolar cation channels, Na+/H+ exchange, and Na+/K+ homeostasis [59,60]. Thus, elevated levels of SOS1–3, NHX1–3, SKOR, HAK5/7, and NRT1.5 in response to salt stress may preserve the K+ concentration and Na+/K+ homeostasis as well as increase the salt tolerance conferred by Spd-CDs regulated ROS accumulation.

Foliar application of Spd-CDs alleviated damage from salt stress in tomatoes in a concentration-dependent effect. Enhanced salt tolerance provided by Spd-CDs was linked to photosynthesis and plant growth in a manner dependent on H2O2. Mechanistically, Spd-CDs induced RBOH1 and PAO which led to the activation of RBOH- and PAO-dependent H2O2 molecule production, and endowed plants with salt resistance (Fig. 10 ). Spd-CDs-promoted H2O2 molecule production positively affected the expression of Fe-related genes and ameliorated Fe deficiency due to salt stress. Spd-CDs also increased Na+- and K+-related transporters and channels, thus maintaining Na+/K+ homeostasis. As noted, Spd-CDs suppressed root-to-shoot Na+ and promoted root-to-shoot K+, dependent on H2O2 molecule production by the RBOH- and PAO-pathways. Thus, re-established ion homeostasis and subsequent salt tolerance were observed in tomatoes. Moreover, applying Spd-CDs in salt-contaminated conditions represents an eco-friendly measure to alleviate salt stress damage in plants and ensure food safety. This study enriches our current understanding of Spd-CDs-induced stress tolerance in plants; however, more research is needed to identify the molecular mechanisms that act downstream to translate Spd-CDs-induced ROS signals to transcriptional levels and beyond. However, currently, limitations in the large-scale and costly synthesis of Spd CDs are hampering the use of Spd CDs in agriculture. As a result, Spd-CDs has great potential in the future of agriculture.
